content.text: Branching and merging are the foundational workflow primitives in Git-based version control. A branch is a lightweight pointer to a commit that allows parallel lines of development to coexist independently. Merging integrates changes from one branch into another through one of several strategies — merge commit, squash, rebase, or cherry-pick — each with different trade-offs for history legibility, collaboration safety, and CI/CD integration.

In Git, a branch is simply a named pointer to a specific commit. Creating a branch costs almost nothing — Git writes a 41-byte file under .git/refs/heads/ containing the target commit SHA. All commits made on a branch advance this pointer automatically, forming an independent chain of development that diverges from the point at which the branch was created.

Merging is the process of combining the commit histories of two branches. Git computes a three-way merge: it identifies the merge base (the common ancestor commit), applies the diff from the merge base to the source branch, and applies the diff from the merge base to the target branch. If the same lines were changed in both, a merge conflict occurs and must be resolved manually.

The commit graph — the directed acyclic graph (DAG) of all commits and their parent relationships — is the central data structure of a Git repository. Understanding how branching creates diverging paths and how merging creates new edges in this graph is essential for effective version control.

Without branching, all developers commit directly to a single shared line — a practice that makes it impossible to develop features in isolation, prevents code review before integration, and means every incomplete change is visible to the entire team. Branching solves all of these problems by creating isolated sandboxes for each feature, bug fix, or experiment.

For AI/ML codebases, branching is especially valuable. Model training experiments, prompt engineering iterations, and infrastructure changes all benefit from isolation. A data scientist can modify a training pipeline on a branch while CI/CD remains green on main. A prompt engineer can test multiple variants on separate branches before deploying the winner.

Merging strategies determine the shape of the commit history — a key artifact for debugging, audit, and blame attribution. Teams with high-quality, linear histories (from squash or rebase merges) can bisect bugs efficiently. Teams with rich merge histories (from merge commits) can trace exactly when features were integrated and by whom.
In-progress features, bug fixes, and experiments are invisible to main until explicitly merged.
Multiple developers can work on different features simultaneously without blocking each other.
Code review, automated tests, and build verification happen on the branch before it reaches main.
Creating and deleting branches is near-instant; dead-end experiments are discarded without affecting the main line.
Long-lived branches (release/, hotfix/) enable parallel maintenance of multiple deployed versions.
Long-lived branches that diverge significantly from main accumulate merge debt; resolving large conflicts is time-consuming.
Many merge commits can make the commit graph difficult to read; rebase-heavy workflows can confuse contributors unfamiliar with Git history rewriting.
Unmaintained stale branches accumulate over time; teams must enforce branch lifecycle policies (auto-delete after merge).
Rebasing published branches requires force-push, which can destroy colleagues' work if not coordinated carefully.

A branch is created from a specific commit (HEAD by default). The new branch's pointer and HEAD's pointer both point to the same commit initially — they diverge as new commits are made on the new branch.

# Create a branch and switch to it (modern syntax)
git switch -c feature/user-auth

# Equivalent legacy syntax
git checkout -b feature/user-auth

# Create from a specific commit
git switch -c hotfix/security-patch main

# Create from a remote branch
git switch -c my-feature origin/main --track
Git supports three primary integration strategies:

# 1. Merge commit (default): creates a new merge commit with two parents
#    Preserves full branch history; non-linear graph
git switch main
git merge feature/user-auth
# Result: M commit with parents C2 (main) and F3 (feature)

# 2. Squash merge: collapses branch into one commit on target
#    Linear history; loses per-commit attribution on feature branch
git merge --squash feature/user-auth
git commit -m "feat: add user authentication"

# 3. Rebase: replays feature commits on top of main
#    Linear history; rewrites commit SHAs
git switch feature/user-auth
git rebase main
git switch main
git merge --ff-only feature/user-auth  # fast-forward only; fails if any merge commit needed

For each file changed in either branch, Git finds the merge base (common ancestor commit) and applies both sets of changes. If file A was modified only in branch 1, it takes branch 1's version. If modified only in branch 2, it takes branch 2's. If modified in both, a conflict is raised and the developer must resolve it manually.

attrs.alt: Git commit graph showing feature branch, hotfix branch, merge commits, squash commits, and release tags
Git branching and merging workflow: feature branch, hotfix branch, and three merge strategies

content.text: On shared, long-lived integration branches (develop, staging) where preserving the full context of each feature branch matters.
When the pull request represents a logical unit of work that should appear as a distinct node in the history.
In workflows where multiple contributors worked on the same branch and individual commit attribution within the feature is important.
When the feature branch has many WIP, 'fix typo', or 'add forgotten file' commits that add noise without value.
When a clean, one-commit-per-feature policy simplifies blame and bisect operations on main.
GitHub pull requests default to squash merge for most enterprise teams for this reason.
When updating a personal feature branch with upstream changes from main before opening a pull request.
When the team enforces a fully linear history policy on main.
When bisect speed is critical — a purely linear history makes binary search maximally efficient.
Never rebase public branches that others have checked out — it rewrites SHAs and forces everyone to re-synchronise.
Do not rebase a branch with a large number of commits if each commit represents a separately deployable unit (prefer merge commit in that case).

# 1. Start from up-to-date main
git switch main
git pull origin main

# 2. Create feature branch
git switch -c feature/llm-streaming

# 3. Develop and commit
git add src/streaming.py tests/test_streaming.py
git commit -m "feat: implement SSE streaming for LLM responses"
git add src/streaming.py
git commit -m "fix: handle disconnect gracefully in streaming endpoint"

# 4. Keep branch current with main (rebase strategy)
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main
# Resolve any conflicts, then:
git rebase --continue

# 5. Push and open PR
git push origin feature/llm-streaming

# 6. After PR approval, merge (squash via GitHub UI, or locally:)
git switch main
git merge --squash feature/llm-streaming
git commit -m "feat: SSE streaming for LLM responses (#142)"

# 7. Clean up
git push origin main
git push origin --delete feature/llm-streaming
git branch -d feature/llm-streaming

# Production bug found; branch from the current release tag
git switch -c hotfix/fix-auth-bypass v2.3.0

# Apply the fix
git commit -m "fix: prevent JWT bypass when alg=none"

# Merge back to main AND the affected release branch
git switch main
git merge hotfix/fix-auth-bypass
git tag v2.3.1
git push origin main --tags

# Also apply to the release branch if it exists
git switch release/2.3
git cherry-pick hotfix/fix-auth-bypass

# Delete hotfix branch
git branch -d hotfix/fix-auth-bypass

# Attempt to merge feature branch; conflict occurs
git merge feature/config-update
# Auto-merging src/config.py
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/config.py
# Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

# Inspect the conflict
git status
# both modified: src/config.py

cat src/config.py
# <<<<<<< HEAD
# MAX_RETRIES = 3
# =======
# MAX_RETRIES = 5
# RETRY_DELAY = 1.0
# >>>>>>> feature/config-update

# Edit src/config.py to resolve:
# MAX_RETRIES = 5       <- take feature value
# RETRY_DELAY = 1.0     <- take new feature line

# Mark as resolved and complete merge
git add src/config.py
git merge --continue
# Opens editor for merge commit message
Example 2 — Interactive rebase to clean history

# Your feature branch has messy commits:
git log --oneline origin/main..HEAD
# a3f9c2 fix typo
# 7bc810 WIP: debugging
# 4d22e1 WIP: start refactor
# 9a01fe feat: initial auth implementation

# Interactive rebase to squash, reword, and drop
git rebase -i origin/main
# Opens editor showing:
# pick 9a01fe feat: initial auth implementation
# pick 4d22e1 WIP: start refactor
# pick 7bc810 WIP: debugging
# pick a3f9c2 fix typo

# Edit to:
# pick 9a01fe feat: initial auth implementation
# squash 4d22e1 WIP: start refactor
# squash 7bc810 WIP: debugging
# squash a3f9c2 fix typo

# Save and close; Git will rebase and open another editor
# for the combined commit message:
# feat: implement user authentication with JWT

# A security fix was committed to develop; need it in the v1.x release branch
git log --oneline develop | grep "security"
# e4f9a12 fix: sanitise SQL inputs in user API (CVE-2024-1234)

# Cherry-pick that commit onto the release branch
git switch release/1.x
git cherry-pick e4f9a12
# [release/1.x 9b2c4d8] fix: sanitise SQL inputs in user API (CVE-2024-1234)
# Note: new SHA assigned (9b2c4d8 ≠ e4f9a12)

git push origin release/1.x
git tag v1.8.2
git push origin --tags

# Cherry-pick a range
git cherry-pick e4f9a12^..b8c31aa  # commits from e4f9a12 to b8c31aa inclusive

A machine learning platform team uses a GitHub Flow variant: all development happens on short-lived feature branches off main, PRs require two approvals and all CI checks (unit tests, model eval, lint, security scan), and merges use squash merge to maintain a linear main history. Release branches are cut from main at sprint end with a naming convention of release/YYYY-MM-DD.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Branch lifecycle script used in CI/CD (simplified)

set -euo pipefail
BRANCH_NAME="${1:?Branch name required}"
BASE_BRANCH="${2:-main}"

# Validate branch name follows convention
if [[ ! "$BRANCH_NAME" =~ ^(feature|fix|chore|experiment)\/.+ ]]; then
    echo "Error: branch name must match (feature|fix|chore|experiment)/<description>" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Create from latest base
git fetch origin "$BASE_BRANCH"
git switch -c "$BRANCH_NAME" "origin/$BASE_BRANCH"

echo "Created $BRANCH_NAME from origin/$BASE_BRANCH"
echo "Push with: git push origin $BRANCH_NAME"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Post-merge cleanup hook (.git/hooks/post-merge or CI step)
# Delete the merged feature branch automatically

MERGED_BRANCH=$(git log --merges -1 --pretty=format:"%s" | grep -oP "(?<=Merge branch ').*(?=')")
if [[ -n "$MERGED_BRANCH" && "$MERGED_BRANCH" != "main" ]]; then
    git push origin --delete "$MERGED_BRANCH" 2>/dev/null || true
    git branch -d "$MERGED_BRANCH" 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "Deleted branch $MERGED_BRANCH"
fi

# WRONG: rebasing after pushing causes history divergence
git push origin feature/my-branch   # pushed to remote
git rebase main                      # rewrites SHAs
git push --force origin feature/my-branch  # forces diverged history on remote
# Colleagues who pulled feature/my-branch now have a broken local copy

# RIGHT: only rebase unpushed commits; or use --force-with-lease instead of --force
git push --force-with-lease origin feature/my-branch
# --force-with-lease fails if someone else pushed after your last fetch
Mistake 2 — Long-lived branches with no rebase policy

A feature branch open for weeks without pulling from main accumulates merge debt. When it finally merges, the conflicts can take hours to resolve. Enforce a policy: feature branches must be rebased on or merged with main at least every 2–3 days.
Mistake 3 — Committing secrets or large files on a branch

Secrets committed to any branch — even a non-merged feature branch — are visible in repository history. Consider all branches public as soon as they are pushed to a remote. Use git filter-repo (not git filter-branch) to scrub secrets from history; the offending credentials must also be rotated immediately.

Stale merged branches accumulate over months, making the branch list confusing and slowing git fetch operations. Enable 'delete branch on merge' in your repository settings on GitHub/GitLab. Run git branch -d on local copies after merging.
Keep feature branches short-lived — aim for branches that can be merged within 1–5 days of creation.
Name branches with a type prefix: feature/, fix/, chore/, hotfix/, experiment/ to communicate intent.
Rebase your feature branch on main (or the target branch) before opening a pull request to minimise merge conflicts.
Never force-push to main, develop, or any other shared long-lived branch.
Use --force-with-lease instead of --force when force-pushing is necessary — it fails if someone else pushed after your last fetch.
Enable branch protection rules: require PR reviews, passing CI, and no direct pushes to main.
Delete branches immediately after merging — automate this via repository settings.
Use descriptive commit messages on feature branches — even if squashed, good messages help reviewers and the final squash message.
Tag every production release on main with a semantic version tag so hotfixes can branch from an exact production state.

Git branch creation and deletion are O(1) operations — they only modify a 41-byte pointer file. Branch switching (checkout) involves updating the working tree, which scales with the number of files that differ between branches. In very large monorepos (millions of files), git sparse-checkout or partial clone reduces the working tree footprint.

Merge operations scale with the size of the diff between branches, not the repository size. Three-way merges are fast for small divergences; large divergences with many conflict regions require proportional human time for resolution. This is why frequent, small merges are operationally cheaper than infrequent, large ones.

For AI/ML codebases with large model files or datasets, avoid committing binary blobs to Git. Use Git LFS (Large File Storage) or store artifacts in an object store (S3, GCS) and reference them by hash in the repository. LFS stores large files in a separate content-addressable store, keeping the Git DAG fast.
GitHub / GitLab branch protection and merge strategies

# GitHub CLI: protect main branch
gh api repos/:owner/:repo/branches/main/protection   --method PUT   --field required_status_checks='{"strict":true,"contexts":["ci/pytest","ci/lint"]}'   --field enforce_admins=true   --field required_pull_request_reviews='{"required_approving_review_count":2}'   --field restrictions=null
git-flow vs GitHub Flow vs trunk-based development
SaaS with release trains, versioned software
Fast-moving teams; main is always deployable
Expert teams; requires strong feature-flag discipline
Teams deploying to multiple environments (staging, prod)

# Visualise branch graph
git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate

# Find the merge base of two branches
git merge-base main feature/my-branch

# List merged branches (safe to delete)
git branch --merged main | grep -v "^* main"

# List unmerged branches
git branch --no-merged main

# Show what changed on a branch vs main
git log --oneline main..feature/my-branch
git diff main...feature/my-branch  # changes introduced by feature (triple dot)

# Find when a file was last changed on a branch
git log --oneline -- path/to/file

Without branch protection, any team member can push directly to main, force-push over colleagues' work, or delete branches with unmerged commits. Enable branch protection rules that require: at least one passing CI status, at least one code review approval, and no bypassing for repository administrators.

Commit signatures (GPG or SSH key signing) verify that a commit was made by the claimed author. In critical repositories, require signed commits on main and release branches to prevent commit spoofing by a compromised developer account.

# Configure GPG signing globally
git config --global user.signingkey YOUR_GPG_KEY_ID
git config --global commit.gpgSign true

# Or SSH signing (newer, simpler)
git config --global gpg.format ssh
git config --global user.signingkey ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
git config --global commit.gpgSign true

Any secret committed to any branch — even a branch that is never merged — is potentially exposed if the repository is ever made public or if an attacker gains read access. Use pre-commit hooks with tools like detect-secrets or Gitleaks to scan for credentials before every commit.

Think of the commit graph as a timeline that can fork. Main is the river. Feature branches are tributaries that branch off, develop in isolation, and eventually flow back into the river. Merge commits are the points where tributaries rejoin. The water (code) in each tributary does not mix with the river until merging.

A rebase is like physically lifting a tributary and replanting its source further downstream on the river. The tributary's water is identical, but it now branches from a more advanced point. The old branch point is abandoned (no merge commit node in the graph).

A squash merge is like pouring all of a tributary's water into a single bucket and dumping that bucket into the river at one point. The individual drops (individual commits) are lost in the mix, but the total water content (the final code change) arrives at the river.
A Git branch is a lightweight pointer to a commit; creating or deleting a branch is O(1) — it writes or removes a 41-byte file.
Three integration strategies: merge commit (preserves history), squash (linear, loses per-commit attribution), rebase (linear, rewrites SHAs).
Keep feature branches short-lived (1–5 days) to minimise merge debt and conflict surface.
Never rebase or force-push branches that others have checked out — always use --force-with-lease as a minimum safeguard.
Enable branch protection rules: require passing CI, at least one review, and no direct pushes to main.
Delete branches immediately after merging — automate this with repository settings.
Rebase your feature branch on main before opening a pull request to minimise reviewer-facing conflicts.
Tag production releases from main with semantic version tags to enable reliable hotfix branching.
